judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 answered better: it caught both the cross-tenant leak and the exports.py runtime break, while Report 1 missed the export breakage entirely.

report_1.evidence: `backend/app/appointments.py`, in `search_appointments` (added function, ~lines 9–19): cross-tenant data leak — the new search query never filters by `tenant_id`.

report_2.evidence: Cross-tenant data leak in the new search endpoint — `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments` ... the raw SQL it builds only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes` — it never adds `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id`

Runtime break in the nightly export job — `backend/app/db.py:7` vs `backend/app/exports.py:9`. ... `backend/app/exports.py`'s `nightly_export` was not updated and still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`

report_1.evidence: Today this is not exploitable for injection because `status` is constrained by FastAPI to the `AppointmentStatus` enum before it reaches this function ... so `status.value` can only ever be one of `"booked"/"cancelled"/"completed"`. I'm flagging this as a secondary/lower-severity ... rather than a standalone blocker

report_2.evidence: (Minor, non-blocking but worth flagging) SQL built with an f-string. ... `status` is constrained by the `AppointmentStatus` enum at the FastAPI layer so this particular field isn't currently injectable
